coredata.dc:description: This paper reports synthesis and characterization of original photocatalytic materials based on g-C3N4 and ZnIn2S4 compounds and doped with carbon dots. Such materials can be used for degradation of organic contaminants and water splitting. Deposition of the ZnIn2S4 phase on surfaces of g-C3N4 materials resulted in a substantial increase in their specific surface area and adsorption capacity. Synthesis of heterostructures in the presence of carbon dots favored formation of uniform structures with larger surface areas and a narrow size distribution of pores. Photocatalytic properties of the materials produced in this work were tested by performing degradation of rhodamine B. The highest photocatalytic activity was demonstrated by the heterostructures with the 25/75 ratio of the g-C3N4 and ZnIn2S4 components. Doping 2.5 w.% carbon dots into the resulting g-C3N4/ZnIn2S4 heterostructures provided an additional 1.3-fold increase in their photocatalytic activity. Carbon dots in the heterostructures contributed to accumulating photoexcited electrons and therefore intensifying dye degradation. Photoexcited electrons were found to mostly participate in rhodamine B deethylation via formation of O2·– radicals. Destruction of the dye structure was found to occur via its interactions with photoexcited holes and partially with the OH· radicals generated by O2·– particles.

row.entry: Relative increase in the photocatalytic activity of triple hybrids

head.ce:title: Dye adsorption and degradation properties of g-C3N4/ZnIn2S4 and g-C3N4/C-dots/ZnIn2S4 photocatalytic materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Kazan National Research Technological University, Kazan 420015, Russia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Kazan National Research Technological University, Kazan 420015, Russia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper reports synthesis and characterization of original photocatalytic materials based on g-C3N4 and ZnIn2S4 compounds and doped with carbon dots. Such materials can be used for degradation of organic contaminants and water splitting. Deposition of the ZnIn2S4 phase on surfaces of g-C3N4 materials resulted in a substantial increase in their specific surface area and adsorption capacity. Synthesis of heterostructures in the presence of carbon dots favored formation of uniform structures with larger surface areas and a narrow size distribution of pores.

Photocatalytic properties of the materials produced in this work were tested by performing degradation of rhodamine B. The highest photocatalytic activity was demonstrated by the heterostructures with the 25/75 ratio of the g-C3N4 and ZnIn2S4 components. Doping 2.5 w.% carbon dots into the resulting g-C3N4/ZnIn2S4 heterostructures provided an additional 1.3-fold increase in their photocatalytic activity. Carbon dots in the heterostructures contributed to accumulating photoexcited electrons and therefore intensifying dye degradation. Photoexcited electrons were found to mostly participate in rhodamine B deethylation via formation of O2·– radicals. Destruction of the dye structure was found to occur via its interactions with photoexcited holes and partially with the OH· radicals generated by O2·– particles.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Development of efficient photocatalytic materials is among the most vibrant research trends of the last decade. To achieve this goal, researchers modify structures of various semiconductor compounds to produce materials that are capable of absorbing and converting energy of UV and visible light.

Among such materials, compounds of graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) are the most promising candidates. They are relatively inexpensive and can be synthesized from widespread components. They are chemically stable and offer tunable optical properties. These photocatalysts are ecologically friendly and non-toxic. They exhibit photocatalytic properties under sunlight [1–5].

Such a water splitting capability by utilizing light energy makes these materials promising candidates for hydrogen energy applications [6–8]. These photocatalysts may contribute to reduction of carbon dioxide emission by transforming CO2 into valuable fuel or chemicals [9,10]. Compounds based on g-C3N4 accelerate degradation of various pollutants and possess antibacterial properties [11–13]. These photocatalysts are also capable of selective acceleration of oxidation and reduction reactions in organic synthesis applications [14,15].

Such compounds absorb visible light. However, their photocatalytic activity is rather low due to intensive recombination of charges. In this respect, g-C3N4 (CN) materials are mostly transformed into heterostructures by combining them with other substances [2,4]. Development of their hybrids with semiconductors favors distribution of charge carriers in different phases and increases lifetimes of charges and the resulting redox activity in reactions with surrounding molecules.

Zinc indium sulfide ZnIn2S4 (ZIS) is a representative of materials that are used for forming heterostructures with CN. This material possesses intrinsic photocatalytic properties in visible light [16–18]. A combination of CN and ZIS compounds provides heterostructures that exhibit Type-I, Type-II and S-scheme heterojunctions and the resulting intensive absorption of visible light and distribution of charge carriers. As compared with individual CN or ZIS semiconductors, such hybrid materials demonstrate better photocatalytic properties in hydrogen synthesis reactions [19,20], reduction of carbon dioxide and other compounds [21–24], degradation of organic pollutants, and antibacterial capabilities [25–28].

Among various approaches to synthesis of ZIS structures, hydrothermal method is the most preferable because of its simplicity, scalability, and the resulting production of nanostructured materials with large specific surface areas.

Another approach to enhancing photocatalytic properties is development of hybrid semiconductor–metal structures (in particular, including noble metal nanoparticles) that provide a transfer of photoexcited electrons to metal and, thus, favor separation of charges. In the last few years, researchers focused on development of non-metallic heterostructures based on carbon materials such as carbon dots and nanodots, graphene oxide, and etc. The working principle of such materials is similar to their metallic counterparts: they play the role of electron acceptors. Non-metallic materials are, however, more accessible and offer a broad-range control of their electronic properties.

For example, the photocatalytic activity of CN structures in rhodamine B degradation reactions was found to be 16.2 % higher in the presence of carbon nanotubes [29]. Another work reports that the use of carbon nanotubes as electron mediators in CN and CdZnS heterostructures provided a 33 % increase in hydrogen yield [30].

Development of CN heterostructures with carbon dots (C-dots) was found to be a more promising approach to enhancing their photocatalytic activity: a 3- fold increase for rhodamine B (RhB) degradation and a 3.7-fold increase for photodegradation of phenol [31]. Such heterostructures also demonstrate their photocatalytic activity in reactions involving degradation of antibiotics and reduction of carbon dioxide. They are capable of simultaneous degradation of contaminants and generation of hydrogen.

Similar to CN, combining ZIS with C-dots enhances photocatalytic properties of the resulting hybrid materials. As compared with individual ZIS compounds, ZIS/C-dot heterostructures provided a 15.7-fold increase in diclofenac degradation rate [32], a 2.3-fold increase in methyl orange degradation rate [33], and a 2.9-fold increase in hydrogen production rate [34].

On the other hand, the literature review revealed lack of papers that report a sufficient characterization of CN/ZIS heterostructures containing carbon dots. Some manuscripts focus on similar topics, such as CN-ZIS heterostructures that contain reduced graphene oxide (rGO) [35], nanocarbon [36], or graphene [37]. It was reported that CN/ZIS heterostructures modified by rGO provided a 1.6-fold increase in triclosan degradation rate in visible light as compared with individual CN/ZIS materials. In [36] and [37], the authors reported a growing intensity of hydrogen generation via water splitting (3.2 times and 2.6 times, respectively) as compared with pure CN/ZIS structures.

The work [38] discusses intensification of RhB degradation in the presence of Zn2V2O7/C-dot/g-C3N4 hybrids: a 1.4-fold increase and a 1.9-fold increase as compared with Zn2V2O7/C-dot and g-C3N4/C-dot heterostructures, respectively. Similarly, the degradation rate of RhB provided by g-C3N4/BiVO4/C-dot heterostructures was found to be nearly 1.7 faster comparing binary g-C3N4/BiVO4 materials [39].

Thus, we can expect that introduction of carbon dots may allow for a substantial enhancement of the photocatalytic activity of CN/ZIS heterostructures both in degradation of various organic pollutants and hydrogen generation.

According to literature, such hybrids can be doped with carbon dots synthesized from a variety of precursors: graphite electrodes [33], starch [34], glucose [38], citric acid and formamide [39], urea [40], plants [41] and etc.

At the same time, some researchers report that an enhanced photocatalytic effect can be achieved with defect-enriched structures that contain defects in both the CN material and C-dots. Such materials can be produced by incorporating atoms of nitrogen, sulphur or other elements into the structure of the respective material [31]. In [40], the authors report that carbon dots enriched with nitrogen perform a more efficient transfer of electrons in a broader range of absorbed light.

This study aims at synthesis of CN/C-dots/ZIS heterostructures, which possess enhanced adsorption and photocatalytic characteristics with respect to rhodamine B degradation under visible light. We synthesized and characterized materials with the optimal ratio of CN and ZIS phases and concentrations of C-dots that demonstrated efficient adsorption and enhanced photocatalytic properties.

The following materials were used for synthesis: melamine (99 %, Vitareactiv, Russia) thioacetamide (99 %, ROD, Russia), zinc acetate (II) dihydrate (99 %, Reahim, Russia), indium acetate (III) (99 %, Sigma Aldrich), rhodamine B (99 %, Lenreactiv, Russia), methylene blue (98 %, Lenreactiv, Russia), 1,4-benzoquinone (99 %, Reahim, Russia), EDTA disodium salt (98 %, Lenreactiv, Russia), isopropyl alcohol (99 %, Vekton, Russia), o-phenylenediamine (99 %, Reahim, Russia), and citric acid monohydrate (99 %, Lenreactiv, Russia).

CN structures were synthesized by thermal decomposition of melamine by performing its heating to 550 °C at the rate of 5 °C/min (2 h) and further one hour exposure at 550 °C. To synthesize CN/ZIS heterostructures (the synthesis procedure is further described for CN/ZIS-75), 50 mg of g-C3N4 were ultrasonically dispersed in 30 ml of distilled water (100 W, 44 kHz) for 10 min. The resulting dispersion was doped with 77.7 mg (0.353 mmol) of zinc acetate dihydrate, 206.1 mg (0.706 mmol) of indium (III) acetate, and 132.6 mg (1.765 mmol) of thioacetamide and agitated at 25 °C for 1 h. The reaction mixture was further hydrothermally processed at 180 °C for 12 h. The compounds with other content of ZIS (0, 25, 50, 75, and 100 w.%) were synthesized according to the same procedure. After completion of synthesis, the reaction products were purified by 4 repeated precipitations from distilled water by centrifugation at 5,000 rpm for 30 min.

Carbon dots were synthesized and purified according to the method described in [42]. 108 mg of o-phenylenediamine and 192 mg of citric acid were dissolved in 17 ml of distilled water. The resulting solution was further autoclaved at 180℃ for 10 h. After cooling, the solution was centrifuged at 8,000 rpm for 30 min and filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane filter.

The heterostructures doped with carbon dots (CN/CD-2.5/ZIS) were synthesized as follows: 25 mg of CN and 2.5 mg of carbon dots were ultrasonically dispersed in 16 ml of distilled water (100 W, 44 kHz) for 20 min. The resulting dispersion was doped with 38.9 mg (0.177 mmol) of zinc acetate dihydrate, 103 mg (0.353 mmol) of indium (III) acetate, and 66.3 mg (0.882 mmol) of thioacetamide and then agitated at 25 °C overnight. Then, the reaction mixture was hydrothermally processed at 180 °C for 12 h. The CN/CD/ZIS heterostructures with other contents of carbon nanostructures were synthesized by introducing 0.25, 1, 2.5, or 5 mg of carbon dots. The resulting heterostructures were purified according to the method described above.

After purification, the samples were kept and used for further photocatalytic studies as aqueous dispersions without subsequent drying. The concentration of photocatalysts in dispersions was measured by drying a portion of dispersion, which was sampled during its intensive agitation.

Surfaces of particles were studied by a Carl Zeiss Evo ls 10 scanning electron microscope; surface images were obtained by the detector of secondary electrons. The elemental composition of surfaces was characterized by EDX analysis. For the SEM studies, 10–15 nm of graphite layer was deposited on the surfaces of the samples by performing thermal vacuum evaporation in the Quorum q150t es apparatus.
The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained on a Hitachi HT7700, Japan. The images were acquired at an accelerating voltage of 100 kV.
Elemental analysis of finely ground powders of the synthesized samples was carried out on a Vario EL CUBE setup by using a Bruker M4 Tornado XRF analyzer.

XPS measurements were performed in a UHV chamber (base pressure ∼ 5 × 10-10 mbar) equipped with Mg KαX-ray source (photon energy hν = 1253.6 eV), and aPhoibos150hemispherical energy analyzer (all from SPECS GmbH). Peak shifts due to any apparent charging were calibrated with the carbon C1s peak set to 284.8 eV.

X-ray power diffraction studies of the samples were performed on a Shimadzu XRD-7000 X-ray diffractometer using monochromatized CuKα radiation (λ = 1.54063 Å). FTIR spectra were obtained on a Bruker ALPHA-T S/N 102,706 Fourier spectrometer. UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded by Perkin Elmer LAMBDA 35 spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence spectra of the aqueous dispersions of the samples with the same absorption intensity (0.15 a.u. at 350 nm) were recorded by Varian Cary Eclipse spectrofluorometer at excitation wavelength 350 nm.

The adsorption capacity of dyes was determined by the method described in [43]. A 100 ml sample of methylene blue (MB) or RhB solution was doped with the aqueous dispersion that contained 10 mg of the studied sample. The mixture agitated intensively for 1 h to reach the adsorption equilibrium. The concentration of the dye was selected in a way to keep 20–50 % of non-adsorbed dye in solution at the state of equilibrium. After that, the solution was kept for 48 h in dark room for precipitation of the catalyst and then filtered through a 0.22 µm syringe filter. The concentration of the dye solution after reaching the equilibrium was determined by spectrophotometry at the respective absorption maximums of the dyes (665 nm for MB and 554 nm for RhB).
Zeta potential of ZIS aqueous suspensions of ZIS was measured on a Malvern Zetasizer Nano-ZS dynamic light-scattering (DLS) analyzer (Malvern Instruments Ltd., Malvern, Worcestershire, UK).

The specific surface areas of CN/ZIS-75 and CN/CD-2.5/ZIS samples were determined from Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) nitrogen absorption isotherms obtained with Nova 1200e, Quantachrome. Average pore sizes of the samples were calculated by Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) method using the absorption and desorption data.

To perform standard photocatalytic studies, 11 ml of distilled water were mixed with 3 ml of the RhB solution (0.001 mol/l) and then doped with pre-calculated volume of dispersed 5 mg of the photocatalyst that was taken from its aqueous dispersion during intensive agitation. Then, more distilled water was added to the reaction mixture to obtain total 15 ml of solution. The solution was agitated in dark room for 30 min (T = 25 °C) to reach the adsorption equilibrium. Photocatalytic studies were carried out using a 30 W LED chip (COB module) with an emission peak of 452 nm as a radiation source (FWHM = 26 nm). The photodegradation was controlled by sampling at certain time intervals. The temperature of the reaction mixture was maintained within 25 ± 0.5 °C. Samples were centrifuged at 3,000 rpm for 15 min to precipitate the photocatalyst suspension.

Photocatalytic recyclability studies were carried out in the same conditions, using 10 mg of the photocatalyst. After each cycle, the photocatalyst was collected and washed by centrifugation for three times.
The CN structures were synthesized by degradation of melamine at 550 °C.

To determine the ratio of CN and ZIS phases corresponding to the optimal photocatalytic performance of the heterostructures, we studied compounds that contained 0 w.%, 25 w.%, 50 w.%, 75 w.%, and 100 w.% of the ZIS phase. The synthesis was carried out according to a proven hydrothermal technique by mixing the pre-synthesized g-C3N4 with zinc, indium, and sulphur compounds.

According to the results of the photocatalytic studies, the optimal ratio of CN and ZIS phases was found to be 25:75. This ratio was further used for synthesis of CN/CD/ZIS heterostructures that contained carbon dots.

Carbon dots were pre-synthesized by the hydrothermal method from o-diphenyl amine and citric acid. The presence of amino groups allowed for creating carbon dots doped with nitrogen, which are more preferable for photocatalytic studies.
The size of the synthesized carbon dots was found to be 2–4 nm with a narrow size distribution. They showed an absorption peak at 370 nm and the intensive luminescence peak at 450 nm (Fig. S1).

To perform a more uniform incorporation of carbon dots into the structure of a CN material, the mixture of precursors was additionally sonicated and thoroughly agitated before performing hydrothermal synthesis.

The composition of the studied samples was confirmed by elemental analysis. The g-C3N4 structures were found to contain excessive amounts of nitrogen atoms with respect to the theoretically calculated value because of the presence of end amino groups (Table S1). These results were also confirmed by the IR method.

According to the EDX data, all the samples with ZIS phase contain Zn, In, and S elements. Their ratios at various surface points was close to the calculated values (Fig. S2). In heterostructures, the signals from C and N that correspond to the g-C3N4 phase were also detected.

Fig. 1 shows the SEM images of the synthesized samples. We can observe a certain initial aggregation in the g-C3N4 sample because of calcination (Fig. 1a). After hydrothermal processing (HTCN), these aggregates decomposed into smaller flaky particles (Fig. 1b).

Hydrothermal synthesis led to formation of finely dispersed submicron laminar ZIS structures (Fig. 1c). Synthesis of CN/ZIS heterostructures favors exfoliation of the initial CN material and deposition on their surface of the ZIS phase which led to formation of hybrid materials with large specific surface areas (Fig. 1d).

C-dots doped into the reaction mixture can be nucleation centers for formation of the ZIS phase during hydrothermal synthesis. Fig. 1e demonstrates that the presence of C-dots led to formation of finer and more uniform heterostructures. Such structures were also found to have thin pins with the 20–50 nm diameter at their bottom, which also increase specific surface areas and adsorption characteristics of the respective materials (Fig. 1f).

According to TEM images (Fig. 1g and 1 h), the CN/CD-2.5/ZIS sample contains light flaky regions, which correspond to thin and uniform layers of the g-C3N4 phase. The darker regions, which correspond to the ZnIn2S4 phase, are in close contact with these layers. The dark regions are non-homogeneous and contain dark 2–5 nm spots, corresponding to carbon dots (Fig. S1).

The diffractogram of the g-C3N4 sample contains the signals at 27.2° which are typical for an ordered structure with the π-π stacking of heptazine fragments (Fig. 2 a). ZIS samples generated variable intensity peaks, which correspond to various planes of the ZnIn2S4 phase according to the PDF-2 database (Ref. No. 03–065-2023). These heterostructures were also found to generate distinctive signals typical for ZnIn2S4.

The IR spectrum of the CN structures contains a broad absorption band at 3000–3600 cm−1 corresponding to hydroxyl groups and amino groups (Fig. 2b). Intensive absorption in the 1200–1650 cm−1 range can be attributed to the C–N bonds in the heterocycle. A narrow peak at 811 cm−1 corresponds to oscillations of triazine groups. The heterostructures demonstrated similar absorption bands typical for CN materials. The signals from triazine groups were found to be weaker for the heterostructures that can be caused by formation of the ZnIn2S4 phase on the surface of g-C3N4 and the resulting reduction of surface functional groups [14]. The IR spectra of the heterostructures show shifting of the triazine peak to the lower frequency range (805 cm−1) as compared with individual g-C3N4 and the second peak appearing at 773 cm−1. Such peaks indicate chemical interactions occurring between various semiconductors and confirm formation of heterostructures (Fig. S3).

The ZIS-100 sample generates the signal in the broad range of 3000–3650 cm−1 and the narrow peak at 1620 cm−1 corresponding to molecules of water and thioacetamide adsorbed on the surface of the catalyst. Such signals were also detected for the studied heterostructures.
Thus, the presence of FTIR spectral bands typical for the CN phase and the diffraction pattern identical to that of the ZIS phase confirm formation of the CN/ZIS and CN/C-dots/ZIS heterostructures.

XPS survey spectra confirm the presence of C and N elements on the surface of the CN sample as well as oxygen from adsorbed water molecules. The ZIS-100 sample generates the signals from Zn, In, and S as well as carbon and oxygen from absorbed molecules of organic compounds and water. Similarly, the presence of all the constituent elements was confirmed for the CN/ZIS and CN/CD-2.5/ZIS heterostructures. The signal from Mo 3d is detected because of the sample holder, which is made of this metal.
The valence bands of the CN and ZIS-100 samples were determined by the XPS method according to Fig. 2d [44–46].

According to the spectra, the CN sample absorbs light in the short-wave range (Fig. 3 a). An increase in the concentration of the ZIS phase in the samples resulted in a more intensive absorption of light in the long wave range due to a smaller band gap of ZIS as compared with CN. Doping heterostructures with carbon dots extends the absorption edge. Due to upconversion features of carbon dots, a broad range of the solar light spectrum (which is not absorbed by the initial components) can be utilized for photocatalytic reactions [47]. The images of the studied samples are shown in Fig. S4.

The band gap values of the studied materials were calculated by Kubelka-Munk method using Tauc plots (Fig. S5). The band gap of the g-C3N4 sample was found to be 2.81 eV. An increase in ZIS content narrows band gap to its minimum of 2.25 eV for 100 % ZIS. The width of the band gap for a direct transition of electrons was found to be 2.4 eV for the CN/CD-2.5/ZIS sample.

The photoluminescence spectra can be useful for characterizing the separation efficiency of the photoexcited charge carriers. The CN sample demonstrates a broad emission peak at 430 nm, which emerges due to recombination of photoexcited electrons and holes (Fig. 3b). A highly developed surface of the ZIS-100 sample contains a large number of defects and charge traps, which can play the role of catalytic centers. Captured by these traps, photoexcited electrons and holes lose their energy due to non-radiative recombination of charges. The resulting effect is zero luminescence. An increase in the ZIS phase concentration in the heterostructures reduces the luminescence intensity. A nonlinear pattern of intensity reduction with varied concentration of the CN phase confirms separation and transition of charge carriers between the components.

The CN/CD-2.5/ZIS sample demonstrates only a weak emission peak from the g-C3N4 phase. Intensive luminescence of the C-dots is no longer observed because of changes in their surface state in the heterostructures [48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dye absorption and specific surface area studies
ce:section.ce:para: Hydrothermal synthesis of ZIS structures produces materials with large specific surfaces. However, separation of synthesis products and their drying may result in agglomeration and aggregation of their microporous structures and, therefore, reduce their adsorption and photocatalytic performance. We characterized the adsorption capacity of the aqueous dispersions of the synthesized compounds (non-dried) by using MB and RhB dyes. [43]. The adsorption data were compared with the results obtained for the samples, which were dried and then ultrasonically re-dispersed.

Specific surface areas of the CN samples were evaluated by adsorption of methylene blue similarly to previous studies [43,49]. The initial g-C3N4 sample was found to have a low specific surface of 22.4 m2/g. Hydrothermal processing did not increase its specific surface. The respective MB adsorption capacity of this sample was found to be also low (Table. 1 ).

Formation of CN/ZIS heterostructures led to a substantial enhancement of adsorption characteristics. 25 % of added ZIS phase provided a 10-fold increase in the adsorption capacity as compared with individual CN material. Higher concentrations of the ZIS phase resulted in further growth of the adsorption capacity of the heterostructures up to its maximum observed for a pure ZIS sample, which are characterized by the largest specific surface areas (Fig. 1c).

Fig. 4 represents the isotherm of methylene blue adsorption by ZIS-100. The figure shows that saturation is reached with 204 mg of the dye adsorbed by 1 g of the sample. This result correlates with the dependence of zeta potential on dye concentration.

A large number of sulphur atoms results in formation of ZIS structures with a high negative surface charge. The zeta potential of the initial ZIS-100 sample was found to be −50.8 mV. Adsorption of MB, which is a cationic dye, neutralizes these surface charges. A complete neutralization occurs with 12 mg/l of added dye and coincides with the adsorption maximum. Higher concentrations of the added dye increase the surface zeta potential up to ∼ 20 mV. This effect may be caused by formation of intermolecular associates of dye molecules on the sample surface [50].

Drying of samples leads to aggregation of surface microstructures and reduction of material’s porosity. For example, the adsorption capacity of MB by CN/ZIS-50 decreases from 159 to 111 mg/g after its drying and subsequent ultrasonic re-dispersion. Fig. S6 shows microscopy images of the sample before and after drying and ultrasonic redispersion. We can see that the dispersity of the sample decreases after its drying because of aggregated microstructures.

Incorporation of carbon dots into the heterostructures resulted in a considerable enhancement (1.6 times) of their adsorption properties (Table 1). The samples with different contents of carbon dots were found to have similar adsorption capacities with respect to MB, indicating that their surface structures can be similar. Formation of structures with better adsorption properties can be explained by their higher surface roughness in the presence of carbon nanoparticles and formation of nanoscale pins, which increase specific surface areas of the samples.

For a more efficient incorporation of C-dots, a longer preliminary sonication and mixing of initial components were performed before hydrothermal synthesis of heterostructures as compared with the original method for synthesis of CN/ZIS materials. Therefore, we suppose that enhanced adsorption properties of the heterostructures with C-dots can also be related to a higher dispersity of CN precursors.
Drying and re-dispersion of these samples in water results in a substantial decrease in their specific surface area. Therefore, non-dried samples were used for photocatalytic studies.
The synthesized substances exhibited high adsorption capacity with respect to RhB, which was further used for photocatalytic studies (Table 1, ACRhB).

The CN/ZIS-75 and CN/CD-2.5/ZIS samples were also used to study adsorption and desorption of nitrogen (Fig. 5 ). The adsorption isotherms correspond to Type IV of Brunauer-Deming-Deming-Teller (BDDT) classification methodology and indicate a mesoporous structure of the samples with pore sizes in the range of 2–50 nm. The Fig. inset shows size distributions of pores in the studied samples determined by the BJH method. In both samples, the peak diameter of pores is about 4.1 nm. The CN/CD-2.5/ZIS heterostructures contain pores with a narrow size distribution, while the size distribution of pores in the CN/ZIS-75 sample is broader.

The specific surface area of the CN/ZIS-75 sample determined by BET was found to be 81.5 m2/g. The CN/CD-2.5/ZIS heterostructures are characterized by a higher SBET value of 95.5 m2/g due to formation of a rougher surface with nanoscale pins (Fig. 1f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of the photocatalytic activity of CN/ZIS materials
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic properties of the produced materials were characterized by performing degradation of RhB under blue LED light (452 nm). Fig. S7 shows the absorbance spectra of the samples depending in the irradiation time and the composition of the photocatalysts.

In the presence of g-C3N4, photocatalytic decomposition of RhB occurred slowly due to intensive recombination of charge carriers on the catalyst surface. The CN/ZIS heterostructures provided a substantial increase in the decomposition rate of this dye. Degradation of RhB shifts its absorption peak from 554 nm to 500 nm due to consecutive deethylation of dye [51,52] and formation of rhodamine 110 (R110) molecules, which are more stable and tend to accumulate in the reaction mixture.

The absorption peak at 554 nm was used to plot and analyze the kinetic curves of RhB degradation (Fig. 6 a). The presence of the catalyst was shown to considerably increase the rate of dye degradation. A substantial decrease in the intensity of the RhB absorption peak was observed for hydrothermally processed samples at the state of their adsorption equilibrium (−30–0 min). Hydrothermal synthesis leads to formation of heterostructures with higher specific surface areas that favor adsorption of dye molecules. It should be noted that dye molecules do not decompose at this stage. They are removed with the catalyst during centrifugation.

Decomposition of adsorbed dye molecules occurs at the initial moment of irradiation, which leads to a rapid decrease in RhB concentration. An additional contribution to a high initial degradation rate can be made by oxygen molecules, which are initially adsorbed on the catalyst surface and participate in the oxidation process.

Fig. 6b represents normalized kinetic curves of RhB degradation by the studied samples. The plot reveals that an increase in ZnIn2S4 content in the heterostructures enhanced photocatalytic activity, which reached its maximum at 75 % of the added ZIS phase.

Photocatalytic degradation of RhB can be described by the first-order reaction equation, which allows to evaluate reaction rate constants from linear plots:
(1) ln(A/A0) = – kt
where A is the intensity of RhB absorption peak at the t time, A0 is the intensity of the RhB peak at the start of reaction.

A decrease in the concentration of the dye at the start of the reaction can be caused by its adsorption on the surface of the catalyst. The reaction rate constant was, therefore, determined from the linear segment of the plot in its medium part (5–45 min from the start of reaction) (Fig. 6c). The reaction rate constants are summarized in Fig. 6d.

Hydrothermal processing of initial g-C3N4 samples changed the reaction rate insignificantly. Formation of CN/ZIS heterostructures led to a substantial increase in the photodegradation rate due to larger specific surface areas of these materials and less probable emissive recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes (Fig. 3b).

The best photocatalytic performance (k = 0.048 min−1) was observed for the CN/ZIS-75 samples. The samples that consist of 100 % ZnIn2S4 possess the largest specific surface area. Their photocatalytic efficiency, however, reduces because of a lower redox potential of the charge carriers.

Similar results were obtained in earlier studies, which reported that the highest photocatalytic activity was achieved with the samples containing 60–85 % of the ZnIn2S4 phase in the CN/ZIS heterostructures [19–21,24–26].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic properties of CN/ZIS heterostructures modified by carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: In the heterostructures modified by carbon dots, the ratio of CN and ZIS phases was 25:75. Such a ratio provided the best photocatalytic effect. The amount of doped carbon dots was 0.25 %, 1 %, 2.5 %, or 5 % of the mass of heterostructures.

Decomposition of RhB by the heterostructures with and without carbon dots were found to occur similarly: both of them involved in deethylation and further degradation of R110 (Fig. S8). Degradation of up to 65 % RhB was achieved at the start of reaction (0)–(2) min) because of large specific surface area of the catalyst, which adsorbed both the molecules of the dye and dissolved oxygen (Fig. 7 a).
Reaction rate constants were calculated from the linear segments of the plots (Fig. S9) in the 4–45 min interval. Their values are summarized in Fig. 7b.

It was found that up to 1 % of added carbon dots did not exert a considerable impact on the rate constant values. The most substantial growth of the rate constant (k = 0.0632 min−1) was provided by 2.5 % of incorporated C-dots. Higher concentrations of carbon dots in the heterostructures (up to 5 %) reduced their photocatalytic activity.

Such a behavior of the photocatalytic activity can be explained by the fact that carbon dots can be electron acceptors, same as metals, and perform a better separation of charge carriers. However, if the content of C-dots is sufficiently high, they can become charge recombination centers.

The experiments performed with CN/CD-1/ZIS samples revealed that drying and subsequent ultrasonic re-dispersion of the photocatalyst led to a 1.44-fold reduction of the dye degradation rate because of aggregation and the resulting lower adsorption capacity.

Table 2 compares our RhB degradation results with the ones obtained for similar CN materials. Our heterostructures with C-dots demonstrated a substantial 4.6-fold increase in the rate of photocatalytic RhB degradation as compared with pure CN materials. Dye degradation rate constants found for the CN/CD-2.5/ZIS heterostructures were 1.3 times higher than the constants calculated for the CN/ZIS-75 materials.

Photogenerated electrons and holes on the catalyst surface interact with molecules of water and adsorbed oxygen and form highly active particles, which interact with dye molecules [54]:
(2) e– + O2 → O2·–,
(3) O2·– + 2H+ +2e– → H2O2,
(4) H2O2 + e– → OH– + OH,
(5) dye + OH· (h+ or O2·–) → degradation products.

An impact of active particles on RhB degradation was studied by radical trapping experiments. Isopropyl alcohol (IPA), Na2EDTA and benzoquinone (BQ) were used as scavengers for OH·, h+, and O2·– active species [55]. The values of the rate constants of these reactions are summarized in Fig. 8 a.

In the presence of isopropyl alcohol, which is an acceptor of OH· particles, no considerable reduction of RhB degradation rate constant was observed. The potential of OH· formation directly from water molecules is higher (2.4 eV) and cannot be reached with the catalysts studied in this work. A certain amount of OH· radicals can be formed in consecutive interactions of electrons with dissolved oxygen molecules.

In the presence of benzoquinone, an almost threefold reduction in the RhB degradation rate was observed. It indicated direct participation of O2·– radicals in dye degradation reactions. According to the absorption spectra of the samples (Fig. 8b), the RhB absorption peak at 554 nm decreased slowly without shifting to a shorter wave range. As partially deethylated molecules of dye generated a peak in a shorter wave range, it is RhB deethylation reaction that is inhibited by benzoquinone and therefore O2·– radicals participate predominantly in this reaction.

Performing RhB degradation in the presence of Na2EDTA, which is an acceptor of the holes (h+), resulted in a fivefold reduction in the degradation rate. According to the absorption spectra, (Fig. 8c), the peak at 554 nm shifted rapidly to 500 nm due to deethylation of RhB molecules. Further degradation of dye molecules in the presence of Na2EDTA occurs, however, much slower. Photoexcited holes, therefore, participate predominantly in the reactions that involve degradation of RhB chromophore structure.

Earlier studies demonstrated that oxygen molecules adsorbed on the catalyst surface are responsible for a substantial initial increase in the degradation rate, which slows down when these molecules are rapidly consumed. We studied photocatalytic degradation of RhB during aeration of the reaction mixture (75 ml/min, clean air) and observed considerable acceleration of the reaction (Fig. 8d). According to the calculation data, the rate constant evaluated for the CN/CD-2.5/ZIS heterostructures was found to be 8 times higher in conditions of aeration and reached 0.54 min−1. A similar acceleration of reaction was observed for the CN/ZIS-75 and ZIS-100 materials, while air bubbling of CN structures did not increase the reaction rate. Saturation of water and, therefore, the surface of the catalyst with air oxygen molecules intensifies deethylation of RhB molecules. According to earlier studies, oxygenation of the reaction mixture accelerates formation of hydrogen peroxide molecules and, in turn, OH· radicals with high oxidizing potential [28]. They increase the rate of R110 decomposition, so a complete degradation of dye is achieved within 30 min (Fig. S10).
The efficiency of charge carrier separation in these heterostructures was evaluated by plotting the energy diagram using band gaps calculated for the individual components.

The values of the valence band obtained for g-C3N4 and ZnIn2S4 by the XPS method were normalized with respect to normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) as follows:
(6) EVB,NHE = ϕ + EVB, XPS − 4.44,
where ϕ is the work function of the XPS instrument (ϕ = 4.392 eV) [45].

The EVB,NHE values calculated for the g-C3N4 and ZnIn2S4 samples (1.71 and 0.59 eV, respectively) were used to determine the edge of the conductivity band (ECB, NHE) as follows:
(7) ECB, NHE = EVB, NHE – Eg,
where Eg is the band gap energy of the semiconductor (2.81 eV for CN and 2.25 eV for ZnIn2S4).

According to the band gap data and radical trapping experiments, we assume that the S-scheme heterojunction occurs in the studied heterostructures [26,56–58]. The energy structure of the synthesized CN/CD/ZIS materials is shown in Fig. 9 . Formation of heterostructures results in a close contact between the phases. Electrons start to migrate from the conductivity band of g-C3N4 (−1,1 eV) to ZnIn2S4 until their Fermi levels become equal and interfacial electric field emerges. The conductivity band of g-C3N4 bends down and the valence band of ZnIn2S4 bends up, respectively. Irradiation by light in the presence of interfacial electric field and Coulomb interactions will lead to a fast recombination of photogenerated electrons from g-C3N4 and holes from ZnIn2S4, which are characterized by weak redox capabilities. The residual photogenerated electrons from ZnIn2S4 and holes from g-C3N4, which are characterized by strong redox capabilities, will migrate to the surface of the heterostructure and participate in dye degradation reactions. Contribution of electrons from the ZnIn2S4 conductivity band (−1,66 eV) to rhodamine B degradation processes via formation of O2·– radicals is confirmed by the kinetic plot that is similar to that of the ZIS-100 sample (Fig. S7d and Fig. S8; Fig. 6b and Fig. 7a).

In the CN/CD/ZIS heterostructures, a transfer of photoexcited electrons of ZnIn2S4 to carbon dots occurs. It provides a more efficient separation of charges and reduces probability of their recombination with the holes [41].

A highly developed surface of the catalyst is capable of absorbing both dye and oxygen molecules, which participate in the degradation reaction. Formation of O2·– radicals on the catalyst surface leads to intensive deethylation of RhB molecules. OH‧ radicals, which form partially from oxygen ion-radicals, also participate in dye degradation. Photoexcited holes, in their turn, participate in dye oxidation reactions and contribute to complete degradation of dye molecules.

According to bandgap characteristics (Fig. 9), the synthesized heterostructures are capable of splitting water to hydrogen and oxygen and performing photoreduction of carbon dioxide in addition to degradation of organic compounds [1].
Regeneration and reuse characteristics of photocatalysts are important for their practical applications. Therefore, we performed repeated photocatalytic experiments with one of the samples.

According to the experimental results, the photocatalytic efficiency of CN/CD-2.5/ZIS sample remained sufficiently high after performing six testing cycles: 85 % of dye degraded within an hour (Fig. 10 a). A major part of dye degraded within 5 min after the start of the reaction. During further 5-minute intervals, the amount of degraded dye decreased due to weaker adsorption behavior of the catalyst and incomplete desorption of degradation products.

The photostability of the CN/CD-2.5/ZIS sample was studied after six cycles of photocatalysis. The SEM image demonstrates a sustainable nanoscale surface pattern of the sample including characteristic nanoscale pins (Fig. 10b). The elemental composition of the surface was studied by the EDX method, which detected all the constituent elements in the analyzed sample (Fig. S11). The average atomic ratio of Zn:In:S elements in the samples was found to be 0.86:2:3.5 (1:2:3.6 before photocatalysis) that indicates a slight tendency of surface zinc ions to dissolution.

In this work, adsorption and photocatalytic properties of heterostructures with various ratios of g-C3N4 and ZnIn2S4 components were characterized in detail by studying degradation of rhodamine B by these catalysts. The adsorption capacity of methylene blue by C3N4/ZnIn2S4-75 heterostructures was found to be 14 times higher than that of g-C3N4 phase due to deposition of the ZnIn2S4 phase with a large specific surface area.

The formation of g-C3N4/ZnIn2S4 heterostructures leads to an increase in their photocatalytic activity up to 3 times compared to pure g-C3N4. The optimal weight ratio of g-C3N4 and ZnIn2S4 phases that provided the highest photocatalytic activity was found to be 25 to 75, respectively.

In this work, we synthesized original heterostructured materials that contain g-C3N4 and ZnIn2S4 phases doped with carbon dots and characterized their adsorption and photocatalytic properties. The presence of carbon dots was found to favor formation of hybrid materials with more uniform structures. The g-C3N4/C-Dots/ZnIn2S4 heterostructures are characterized by larger specific surface areas (SBET = 95.5 m2/g) and narrow size distribution of pores within 2–10 nm that resulted in a 1.6-fold increase in their adsorption capacity as compared with C3N4/ZnIn2S4-75 samples.

Carbon dots attract photoexcited electrons and therefore contribute to a more efficient separation of charge carriers. Doping C3N4/ZnIn2S4 heterostructures with 2.5 w.% carbon dots resulted in an additional 1.3-fold increase in the photocatalytic activity of the hybrids.

We found that separation of charge carriers in the C3N4/C-Dots/ZnIn2S4 occurs according to the S-scheme heterojunction. The mechanisms of interactions between charge carriers and dye molecules were studied. Photoexcited electrons were shown to predominantly participate in RhB deethylation reactions via formation of O2·– radicals. Decomposition of the chromophore structure occurred via interactions with photoexcited holes and partially with OH· radicals that formed from O2·– particles. Carbon dots in the heterostructures favored accumulation of photoexcited electrons and increased dye degradation rates.
The synthesized photocatalysts were confirmed to be stable substances. They possess enhanced photocatalytic properties and can be reused in cyclic experiments.
